name: Summit of Mount Antero
short_description: High-altitude alpine rockhounding on public BLM land at 14,147 feet. Mount Antero is famous for beautiful aquamarine and topaz crystals found in pegmatite outcrops near the summit.
description:
Mount Antero stands as one of Colorado's premier high-elevation rockhounding destinations, attracting mineral collectors from across the country. Located in the Sawatch Range at 14,147 feet elevation, this 14er offers public BLM access with no permits required for personal use collection. The mountain's north slope contains exposed pegmatite bodies that host excellent specimens of aquamarine, smoky quartz, topaz, and colorless beryl.

The geological story of Mount Antero is rooted in the Proterozoic-age Sawatch granite, which was intruded by pegmatite dikes during late-stage crystallization. These pegmatites formed as water-rich melts cooled slowly, allowing large crystals to develop. The aquamarine crystals here can reach several inches in length and display the characteristic pale blue-green color that makes them highly prized by collectors. The site has been actively rockhounded for over a century, with some of the finest Colorado aquamarine specimens coming from this peak.

Expect a strenuous high-altitude experience. The combination of extreme elevation and moderate terrain demands good physical fitness and acclimatization. Most collectors focus their efforts on exposed pegmatite areas on the northeast and north faces, where weathering has loosened crystals from the host rock. The crystal-bearing zones are scattered across several hundred feet of elevation, so patience and careful scanning of the rocky talus and outcrops yield the best results.

Visiting Mount Antero requires early season planning and weather monitoring. The approach involves driving to a trailhead and hiking approximately 3-4 miles with 2,500+ feet elevation gain. Many collectors make this a two-day expedition to maximize time at the summit mineral zones. The rewards for experienced rockhounds are substantial - quality aquamarine and topaz specimens are regularly found by those who invest the effort.
history: Mount Antero's pegmatites formed during the Proterozoic era when granitic magma intruded the Sawatch Range basement rock, with mineral-rich fluids depositing gem-quality crystals as the pegmatite cooled over millions of years. The site has been known to Colorado mineral collectors since at least the 1890s, and remains one of the state's most productive sources of aquamarine and alpine topaz.
tips: Focus your search on exposed pegmatite outcrops on the north and northeast faces of the summit block - the white to tan feldspar matrix is easy to spot against the darker surrounding granite. Bring a pry bar or small pickax to carefully extract crystals from the pegmatite; many of the best specimens are still partially embedded in the matrix rather than loose in the talus. Look for the characteristic light blue color of aquamarine crystals, which often occurs in clusters or groups within the pegmatite. Start your collection early in the day to maximize high-altitude time before weather deteriorates - afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer, and the exposed summit offers no shelter.
terrain_notes: Extremely rugged high-altitude alpine terrain at 14,147 feet elevation. Rocky talus slopes dominate the upper sections with exposed granite and pegmatite outcrops. The approach involves moderate to steep hiking with significant elevation gain. Footing is uneven and loose in many areas. Weather exposure is severe - the summit receives full sun with no tree cover. Winds can be intense, and afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly during summer months. Atmospheric conditions at this elevation can cause fatigue and altitude effects in unacclimatized hikers.
written_directions: From Salida, drive east on US 50 for approximately 30 miles toward Poncha Pass. Exit south on County Road 162 near Maysville toward the Mount Antero trailhead. Follow the rough dirt forest service road for about 6 miles (high-clearance vehicle recommended) to the parking area near the Mount Antero-Shavano trailhead. The actual rockhounding occurs on the upper mountain above treeline, accessible only by hiking the well-established trail approximately 3-4 miles to the summit block, gaining over 2,500 feet elevation.
road_conditions: High-clearance vehicle recommended for forest service road approach. Final 6 miles is rough dirt road with potential washboard and ruts. May require AWD in wet conditions. High elevation and exposure to weather.
parking_notes: Parking area at Mount Antero-Shavano trailhead. Limited pullout space, typically accommodates 8-12 vehicles. No facilities. Arrive early on weekends as this is a popular 14er peak.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations on BLM land. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Specimens must be hand-collected. All rockhounding must occur in designated public access areas. Leave no trace - fill holes and restore the area. This is a 14,000+ foot peak; be prepared for extreme weather and altitude hazards.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation or power tools
Hand-collection only
Leave no trace practices required
Stay on established trails where possible
nearest_services: Salida, Colorado, approximately 35-40 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and emergency care available.
accessibility_notes: Extremely difficult access. Requires substantial hiking ability and altitude acclimatization. Elevations above 14,000 feet create significant physiological stress. Uneven talus terrain with loose rock throughout. No wheelchair or limited-mobility access possible. Steep sections with drop-offs require sure-footedness and scrambling ability. This location is suitable only for experienced hikers in excellent physical condition.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Mount Antero in Colorado?
faq.answer: Mount Antero is famous for aquamarine, smoky quartz, topaz, and beryl crystals found in pegmatite outcrops. Aquamarine is the most prized find here, with light blue specimens ranging from small crystals to several inches in length. These gems are found in the pegmatite matrix on the north-facing slopes near the 14,147-foot summit.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Mount Antero?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on this BLM public land. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations at no cost. However, this is not a fee dig site - you must hike to the summit area and extract specimens yourself from the pegmatite outcrops.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Mount Antero?
faq.answer: July through September is the best rockhounding season at Mount Antero. Winter snow makes access impossible, and spring brings unpredictable weather and snow patches above treeline. Summer offers the most stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms are common. Early July is ideal as snowmelt is complete and the mountain is fully accessible.

faq.question: How difficult is the hike to Mount Antero rockhounding areas?
faq.answer: This is an expert-level rockhounding destination. The hike to the summit gains 2,500+ feet over 3-4 miles at extreme elevation. You must be in excellent physical condition and acclimatized to 14,000+ foot elevations. The terrain involves loose talus, scrambling, and exposure. Beginner rockhounders should seek lower-elevation Colorado sites instead.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect aquamarine at Mount Antero?
faq.answer: Bring a pry bar, small pickax, or cold chisel to extract crystals from the pegmatite matrix. Many of the best aquamarine and topaz specimens are still partially embedded in feldspar. A rock hammer, chisels, and a sturdy backpack are essential. Hand tools only - no motorized excavation is allowed. Bring gloves to protect from sharp pegmatite edges.

faq.question: Can beginners rockhound at Mount Antero, and how much does it cost?
faq.answer: Mount Antero is not suitable for beginners due to extreme elevation, difficult terrain, and altitude hazards. There is no fee to rockhound on this BLM land, but the physical and technical demands are expert-level. Beginners should start at lower-elevation Colorado rockhounding sites. If you are experienced with 14ers and high-altitude hiking, Mount Antero offers rewarding aquamarine and topaz collecting.

meta_description: Collect aquamarine, topaz, and beryl at Mount Antero's 14,147-foot summit. BLM public land, no permit needed. Expert hikers only. Colorado's premier alpine gem location.
